children.name: Hyla
children.description: Small green tree frogs with adhesive toe pads enabling climbing on smooth surfaces. The European Tree Frog is Sweden's rarest amphibian, persisting only in a handful of sites in southernmost Skåne, where it clings to reed stems and calls loudly from pond margins on warm evenings.

children.name: Hyla arborea
children.description: Sweden's rarest amphibian; brilliant green with dark lateral stripe; adhesive toe pads; calls from May to July; IUCN Least Concern but severely declining in northern range.

children.name: Hyla cinerea
children.description: A slender bright-green frog of the southeastern United States, often with a pale lateral stripe. Abundant around ponds and swamps; the state amphibian of Georgia and Louisiana. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Hyla meridionalis
children.description: A green tree frog of the western Mediterranean lacking the dark flank stripe of H. arborea. Found in Iberia, southern France, and northwest Africa. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Hyla intermedia
children.description: An Italian endemic tree frog formerly considered part of H. arborea. Inhabits wetlands, rice paddies, and pond margins across the Italian peninsula. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Hyla savignyi
children.description: A small green tree frog of the Middle East and Caucasus, ranging from Turkey and Cyprus east to Iran. Resembles H. arborea but is genetically distinct and occupies drier, more rocky habitats along streams and irrigation channels.

children.name: Hyla orientalis
children.description: A recently recognised species split from H. arborea, found from the Balkans and Ukraine east through Turkey and the Caucasus. Distinguished by call frequency and genetic markers; inhabits lowland wetlands, marshes, and riverine forest edges.

children.name: Hyla molleri
children.description: An Iberian endemic split from H. arborea on the basis of call and genetic differences. Occupies shrubby pond margins, olive groves, and vineyards across Portugal and most of Spain, where it is the dominant native tree frog.

children.name: Hyla annectans
children.description: A tree frog of the Himalayan foothills and Southeast Asian highlands, found from northeast India and Bangladesh through Myanmar to southwestern China. It bridges the Old World Hyla arborea group and East Asian lineages, hence the name annectans ('connecting').

children.name: Hyla simplex
children.description: A small green tree frog restricted to Hainan Island, China, and parts of the Guangdong coast. It inhabits forest edges, bamboo groves, and agricultural areas near standing water, calling from elevated perches during the monsoon season.

children.name: Hyla chinensis
children.description: A small green tree frog from southeastern China and Taiwan with a pale lateral line.

children.name: Hyla sanchiangensis
children.description: From mountain streams in Fujian province; call frequency differs from H. chinensis.

children.name: Hyla immaculata
children.description: A plain green tree frog from central China without lateral stripes.

children.name: Hyla tsinlingensis
children.description: The most cold-tolerant Hyla in Asia, breeding in temporary pools after snowmelt.

children.name: Dryophytes
children.description: North American and East Asian tree frogs recently separated from Hyla on molecular grounds. Many members of this genus have remarkable cold-tolerance, surviving winter by producing cryoprotectants in their tissues.

children.name: Dryophytes versicolor
children.description: A tetraploid tree frog of eastern North America that is nearly identical in appearance to its diploid sister species D. chrysoscelis. The two are distinguished by call — versicolor's trill is slower — and can co-occur in the same pond. Freeze-tolerant, surviving temperatures well below 0 °C.

children.name: Dryophytes chrysoscelis
children.description: The diploid counterpart to D. versicolor, ranging across central and eastern North America. Its faster trill is the primary field mark separating it from the larger-range versicolor. Able to survive partial freezing and overwinters under leaf litter.

children.name: Dryophytes femoralis
children.description: A small slender tree frog of the longleaf pine flatwoods and wet savannas of the southeastern United States. Its yellow-orange spots on the hidden thigh surface and rapid 'Morse-code' call are distinctive. Closely tied to open pine habitats threatened by fire suppression.

children.name: Dryophytes avivoca
children.description: A cryptic gray-green tree frog of bottomland swamps and cypress sloughs in the southeastern United States. Its call is a rapid, birdlike whistle that rises slightly at the end — entirely unlike other North American Dryophytes. Males call from branches directly above water.

children.name: Dryophytes andersonii
children.description: One of North America's most range-restricted tree frogs, found only in the acidic, sandy-soiled Pine Barrens of New Jersey and a few disjunct populations in North Carolina and the Florida panhandle. Its vivid green back, lavender side stripe, and orange-spotted thighs make it one of the continent's most beautiful frogs.

children.name: Dryophytes walkeri
children.description: A poorly known tree frog of pine-oak forests and montane cloud forest in the Sierra Madre Occidental of western Mexico. Recently elevated to full species status from synonymy with related Dryophytes taxa; limited distributional data exists.

children.name: Dryophytes japonicus
children.description: A common tree frog of Japan, Korea, and northeastern China, frequently found in rice paddies and wetlands. Famous in Japan as a rain-forecaster of folk tradition, its calls intensify before precipitation. Subject to one of the longest-running amphibian monitoring programmes in Asia.

children.name: Dryophytes suweonensis
children.description: A critically endangered tree frog endemic to rice paddy habitats around Suweon and a handful of central Korean lowland sites. Nearly indistinguishable from D. japonicus by sight, it is identified by its slower, lower-pitched call. Rapid agricultural intensification has reduced its range to a few dozen known populations.

children.name: Dryophytes suianeus
children.description: A Korean endemic separated from D. japonicus by its distinct advertisement call.

children.name: Pseudacris
children.description: Chorus frogs of North America.

children.name: Pseudacris crucifer
children.description: The defining sound of North American spring; only 3 cm long; X-shaped marking on back; chorus audible a kilometre away.

children.name: Pseudacris regilla
children.description: Most frequently heard frog in western North America; Hollywood soundtrack standard.

children.name: Pseudacris triseriata
children.description: A small chorus frog of central North America marked with three dark dorsal stripes. Its rising trill, like running a finger over a comb, is among the first amphibian sounds of spring.

children.name: Pseudacris cadaverina
children.description: A cryptic gray-brown frog of rocky streams in southern California and Baja. Its mottled skin matches granite boulders almost perfectly, making it nearly invisible when still.

children.name: Pseudacris clarkii
children.description: A small chorus frog of the Great Plains, from Kansas south to Texas, characterised by irregular green spots on a gray or white background. Breeds explosively in temporary pools after spring and autumn rains on grasslands and prairies.

children.name: Pseudacris brachyphona
children.description: A small chorus frog restricted to forested uplands and hillside seeps in the Appalachians and Cumberland Plateau. Distinguished by a reversed parenthesis-like marking on its back. An early spring breeder, its raspy call echoes from roadside ditches and woodland pools.

children.name: Pseudacris kalmi
children.description: Pseudacris kalmi, the New Jersey chorus frog, is a species of frog in the treefrog family Hylidae. It is found in the states near New Jersey in the United States. It was first described as subspecies of Pseudacris triseriata but it is differentiated by range and size.

children.name: Pseudacris nigrita
children.description: A widely distributed chorus frog of the southeastern coastal plain, ranging from North Carolina to eastern Texas. The four recognised subspecies vary in dorsal pattern from striped to spotted. Breeds in shallow, ephemeral wetlands amid longleaf pine forests.

children.name: Pseudacris ornata
children.description: One of the most colourful chorus frogs, with bold dark dorsolateral stripes, a dark mask, and often reddish-orange on the sides — a striking frog for its small size. Breeding aggregations in southeastern US flatwoods ponds produce a deafening metallic chorus on winter nights.

children.name: Pseudacris ocularis
children.description: The smallest frog in North America at just 2 cm.

children.name: Pseudacris feriarum
children.description: A chorus frog of the US Piedmont with three dark back stripes.

children.name: Acris
children.description: Cricket frogs of North America — small, agile, semi-aquatic hylids that leap prodigiously across the water surface. Despite their family placement they have reduced toe pads and spend most time at pond margins.

children.name: Acris crepitans
children.description: One of the most abundant frogs in North America, yet populations in the northern part of the range (Great Lakes, New England) have collapsed dramatically since the 1970s for poorly understood reasons. Its rapid clicking call resembles two pebbles being struck together. Despite the family, it rarely climbs.

children.name: Acris blanchardi
children.description: A recently elevated species from the central Great Plains, split from Acris crepitans based on morphological and genetic differences. It occupies stream margins, lake shores, and wet grasslands from Nebraska and Kansas south to Texas, where it forms boisterous breeding choruses in summer.

children.name: Osteopilus
children.description: Caribbean tree frogs, the largest genus of hylids native to the West Indies. Several species have bony skull co-ossification with the skin — the basis of the generic name ('bone pilus', referring to the bony head).

children.name: Osteopilus septentrionalis
children.description: The largest tree frog in North America, native to Cuba, the Bahamas, and the Cayman Islands but now widely invasive in Florida. An aggressive predator that eats other frogs, lizards, insects, and small snakes. Its skin secretions cause intense irritation to mucous membranes of humans and predators alike.

children.name: Osteopilus brunneus
children.description: A Jamaican endemic tree frog inhabiting montane forests, bromeliads, and rocky streamsides in the Blue Mountains and John Crow Mountains. It breeds in water-filled bromeliads and rock crevices; its restricted range and deforestation pressure make it Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List.

children.name: Osteopilus pulchrilineatus
children.description: A Hispaniolan endemic found in both the Dominican Republic and Haiti, distinguished by bold dorsolateral stripes running from snout to groin. Inhabits lowland and mid-elevation forests and scrub, often taking refuge in bromeliads and tree hollows during the day.

children.name: Smilisca
children.description: Mexican and Central American tree frogs of lowland forest and seasonal pools.

children.name: Smilisca baudinii
children.description: A large, blotchy brown-and-green tree frog ranging from southern Texas through Central America. Aestivates buried in mud during the dry season and emerges to breed explosively with the first rains.

children.name: Smilisca puma
children.description: A medium-sized tree frog of Caribbean lowland forests from Honduras to Panama. Patterned in tans and browns with darker mottling, it is cryptic against bark during the day. Breeds in temporary pools and flooded forest clearings during heavy rains.

children.name: Smilisca sordida
children.description: A uniformly brown or olive tree frog of Pacific slope forests from southern Mexico to Ecuador, notable for its lack of bold markings. Breeds prolifically in roadside ditches and banana plantation pools; tolerates disturbed habitats better than most Smilisca relatives.

children.name: Smilisca phaeota
children.description: A large, boldly patterned tree frog with dark cross-bands on a greenish-brown background, found from Nicaragua to northwest Ecuador. Inhabits humid lowland forests near rivers and ponds; the distinctive banding pattern breaks up its outline against dappled foliage.

children.name: Agalychnis
children.description: Spectacular Neotropical tree frogs.

children.name: Agalychnis callidryas
children.description: The red-eyed tree frog or red-eyed leaf frog is a species of tree frog in the subfamily Phyllomedusinae native to forests of Central America and northwestern South America. One of the most recognizable frog species, A. callidryas is known for its vibrant coloration, having a bright green body with blue and yellow stripes on the side, a white underside, bright red- to orange-colored feet, and its namesake bright red eyes.

children.name: Agalychnis moreletii
children.description: A Central American leaf frog with red eyes and orange flanks, distinguished from the red-eyed tree frog by its darker body. Critically Endangered, hit hard by chytrid fungus and habitat loss.

children.name: Agalychnis spurrelli
children.description: A leaf frog of Central America and northwest South America able to parachute between trees using extensively webbed feet. Breeds in explosive mass aggregations over forest pools.

children.name: Agalychnis lemur
children.description: A small, nocturnal leaf frog of cloud forest in Costa Rica and Panama, named for its large eyes resembling a lemur's. Critically Endangered following dramatic declines attributed to chytrid fungus; captive assurance colonies exist in several zoos. Its colour shifts from lime-green at night to brown by day.

children.name: Agalychnis dacnicolor
children.description: A large, stout leaf frog of the Pacific slope dry forests of western Mexico, from Sonora south to Oaxaca. Unlike the more arboreal congeners, it often shelters in bromeliads and rocky crevices during the dry season. Notable for explosive breeding activity in temporary pools following the onset of summer rains.

children.name: Phyllomedusa
children.description: Leaf frogs of South America that walk slowly along branches rather than hopping, and whose skin secretions have been studied for medical peptides.

children.name: Phyllomedusa bicolor
children.description: A large green Amazonian leaf frog whose skin secretion ('kambô') is used in Indigenous ritual and contains bioactive peptides studied by pharmacologists. Walks deliberately, hand over hand, through the canopy.

children.name: Phyllomedusa sauvagii
children.description: A frog of the dry Gran Chaco that secretes a waxy lipid coating which it wipes over its body to prevent water loss, allowing it to survive in arid scrub. Excretes uric acid like a reptile to conserve water.

children.name: Phyllomedusa tarsius
children.description: A robust leaf frog of Amazonian lowland rainforest in Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, and Brazil, named for its large eyes and slow, deliberate walking gait reminiscent of a tarsier primate. Lays large egg clutches in leaves folded over water-filled tree holes; the waxy skin reduces desiccation.

children.name: Phyllomedusa hypochondrialis
children.description: A widespread South American leaf frog with distinctive orange and black barring on the flanks and hidden limb surfaces. Ranges from Venezuela and Trinidad south to Bolivia and Brazil; tolerates seasonally dry Cerrado and gallery forest habitats alongside humid Amazonian forest edges.

children.name: Phyllomedusa azurea
children.description: A leaf frog of the Brazilian Cerrado and adjacent Chaco regions in Bolivia and Argentina, related to the waxy monkey frog but inhabiting moister gallery forests and wetland margins. Its name references the pale blue or lilac flanks visible when the animal is handled.

children.name: Phyllomedusa vaillantii
children.description: Constructs leaf nests over water across the Amazon basin.

children.name: Dendropsophus
children.description: Small Neotropical tree frogs, formerly part of Hyla, characterised by their tiny size and yellow-spotted hidden surfaces.

children.name: Dendropsophus ebraccatus
children.description: A tiny Central and South American frog notable for flexible egg-laying, depositing eggs on leaves or directly in water depending on predation risk and conditions. A model species for the study of phenotypic plasticity.

children.name: Dendropsophus minutus
children.description: One of the most widespread South American frogs, a tiny tan species heard chirping around ponds and ditches across the continent. Likely a complex of several cryptic species.

children.name: Dendropsophus microcephalus
children.description: A tiny yellow and brown tree frog of lowland forests from Mexico to Peru, one of the most abundant amphibians in Central American rainforests. Its disproportionately small head gives it a distinctive profile. Highly vocal, forming enormous choruses around ponds on humid nights.

children.name: Dendropsophus leucophyllatus
children.description: A boldly patterned Amazonian tree frog with an intricate map-like pattern of yellow, brown, and white on the dorsum, rendering it cryptic against lichen-covered bark. Widespread in flooded forest edges and riparian habitats across the Amazon basin.

children.name: Dendropsophus nanus
children.description: One of the smallest hylids in South America, this tiny brown frog inhabits seasonal grasslands, marshes, and gallery forest from eastern Brazil through Bolivia, Paraguay, and Argentina. It calls from emergent vegetation at pond margins and tolerates the xeric Chaco.

children.name: Dendropsophus elegans
children.description: A slender, elegantly patterned tree frog of the Atlantic Forest of Brazil, where it inhabits forest clearings, degraded habitat margins, and restinga scrub. Its dorsum bears contrasting dark and pale brown stripes that give it a distinctive appearance among sympatric dendropsophine frogs.

children.name: Scinax
children.description: A speciose genus of small, agile snouted tree frogs across the Neotropics.

children.name: Scinax ruber
children.description: A widespread, highly adaptable tree frog of South America and the Caribbean, often colonising disturbed and urban habitats. Recognised by reddish thighs flashed when it leaps.

children.name: Scinax boulengeri
children.description: Scinax boulengeri is a species of frog in the family Hylidae. It is found in Colombia, Costa Rica, Nicaragua, Panama, and possibly Honduras. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests, intermittent freshwater marshes, pastureland, plantations, rural gardens, and urban areas.

children.name: Scinax fuscovarius
children.description: A highly variable snouted tree frog of the Brazilian Cerrado and surrounding open habitats in Bolivia, Paraguay, and Argentina. One of the most commonly encountered urban frogs in central Brazil, adapting readily to gardens, water tanks, and artificial ponds.

children.name: Scinax crospedospilus
children.description: A snouted tree frog of the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil, characterised by a fringed or scalloped dorsolateral stripe. Inhabits forest-edge vegetation and gallery forest streams in São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, and adjacent states.

children.name: Scinax eurydice
children.description: A small snouted tree frog of Bahia state, Brazil, found in Atlantic Forest remnants and restinga coastal scrub. Named after the mythological figure Eurydice; distinguished by its yellow-orange groin and thigh colouration and a distinctive call pattern.

children.name: Scinax nasicus
children.description: A pale, pointed-snouted tree frog of the dry Gran Chaco of Argentina, Paraguay, and Bolivia. Exceptionally drought-tolerant among hylids, it aestivates under bark and in tree holes during the long dry season, emerging to breed in temporary savannas pools with the summer rains.

children.name: Scinax acuminatus
children.description: A Chaco specialist with sharp snout adapted for burrowing into mud during dry season.

children.name: Boana
children.description: The largest hylid genus in South America, formerly part of a broadly defined Hyla. Boana frogs span virtually all Neotropical habitats, from Amazonian flooded forest to montane cloud forest and dry Cerrado.

children.name: Boana punctata
children.description: A striking tree frog of Amazonian flooded forests and riverine habitats, recognisable by its bright green body densely covered in round yellow spots. Fluoresces blue-green under ultraviolet light — one of the first frogs confirmed to be naturally fluorescent, which may enhance communication in dim forest light.

children.name: Boana raniceps
children.description: One of the largest tree frogs in South America, with a flat frog-like head and robust build. Widespread from the Amazon to the Río de la Plata basin in Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, and Argentina; often encountered in edge habitats, flooded savannas, and gallery forest ponds.

children.name: Boana geographica
children.description: A large tree frog of floodplain and gallery forests throughout Amazonia and the Orinoco basin, named for the map-like network of dark lines on its dorsum. Adults perch on vegetation overhanging slow-moving streams and float ponds, dropping into water when disturbed.

children.name: Boana faber
children.description: A large, robust tree frog of the Atlantic Forest of Brazil and Argentina, famous for constructing circular mud nests at pond margins in which eggs and larvae develop — a behaviour that earned it the name 'ferreiro' (blacksmith). One of the largest hylids, males reaching nearly 10 cm.

children.name: Boana crepitans
children.description: A common and widespread tree frog of northern South America and Trinidad, found in lowland forest clearings, savannas, and disturbed habitats near ponds. Males call from elevated perches and engage in competitive calling bouts; the species is tolerant of human-modified landscapes.

children.name: Boana albopunctata
children.description: A pale brown or gray tree frog with rows of small white spots along the flanks, widespread in open and gallery forest habitats of the Brazilian Cerrado and cerradão. Highly tolerant of seasonal drought and capable of breeding in a wide variety of standing water bodies from cattle ponds to flooded road margins.

children.name: Boana fasciata
children.description: A striped Amazonian species found in flooded forest and oxbow lake margins.

children.name: Boana calcarata
children.description: Distinguished by sharply pointed head and white dorsolateral stripe.

children.name: Pithecopus
children.description: Phyllomedusine leaf frogs of South America, characterised by waxy skin secretions and slow, deliberate arboreal locomotion. Recently separated from Phyllomedusa on molecular grounds.

children.name: Pithecopus azureus
children.description: A vividly coloured leaf frog of the Gran Chaco and adjacent Cerrado in Bolivia, Brazil, and Paraguay, with electric blue and black barring on the hidden limb surfaces. Related to the waxy monkey frog, it also secretes waxy lipids to prevent desiccation. The bold side pattern is exposed as a startle display when the frog opens its legs.

children.name: Pithecopus rohdei
children.description: A leaf frog of the Atlantic Forest of coastal Brazil, from Bahia south to São Paulo, inhabiting restinga scrub and forest edge vegetation near seasonal ponds. Characterised by pale flanks with white spots; lays eggs in leaves folded over breeding pools.

children.name: Pithecopus megacephalus
children.description: A robust leaf frog of the Cerrado and Caatinga dry forests of central and northeastern Brazil, with a notably wide, flat head. One of the larger Pithecopus species, it inhabits gallery forest margins and breeds in temporary pools that form during summer rains in the seasonally dry interior.

children.name: Pithecopus rohrii
children.description: A leaf frog from the Andes of Bolivia and Peru with colourful flanks.

children.name: Pithecopus palliatus
children.description: Found in the Amazon basin; builds leaf nests over hanging water.

children.name: Tlalocohyla
children.description: Small tree frogs of Central America named after Tlaloc, the Aztec rain deity. Recently separated from Hyla, they inhabit lowland tropical forests and are often associated with bromeliads and seasonal pools.

children.name: Tlalocohyla loquax
children.description: A small, loquacious tree frog of lowland Caribbean forests from Mexico to Panama, living in bromeliads and leaf axils. Its name, 'loquax', Latin for talkative, refers to the persistent, rapid calling of males which can continue for many hours at a time.

children.name: Tlalocohyla smithii
children.description: A tiny tree frog of Pacific slope dry forests in Mexico and Guatemala, one of the smallest hylids in the region at under 2.5 cm. It shelters in bromeliads and dry-season vegetation, breeding in temporary pools that form with the onset of the wet season.

children.name: Triprion
children.description: Casque-headed tree frogs of Central America and Mexico. The skull roof is co-ossified with the overlying skin, forming a hard casque used to plug bromeliad water tanks or tree-hole entrances against predators and desiccation — a phenomenon known as phragmosis.

children.name: Triprion spatulatus
children.description: A remarkable tree frog of Pacific Mexico with a spatula-shaped, bony head used to block bromeliad openings or tree holes during the dry season — a strategy called phragmosis. One of the most morphologically unusual hylids, the bizarre casque gives males a distinctive silhouette on the branch.

children.name: Triprion petasatus
children.description: A casque-headed tree frog of the Yucatán Peninsula and adjacent Belize and Guatemala, named for the broad, flat casque resembling a petasus hat. Inhabits seasonally dry tropical forests and thorny scrub, where it plugs tree cavities with its head during the long dry season to prevent desiccation.

children.name: Litoria
children.description: Diverse Australasian tree frogs.

children.name: Litoria caerulea
children.description: The iconic dumpy tree frog; calm temperament; deposits lipid on skin to reduce water loss; enormously popular in captivity.

children.name: Litoria infrafrenata
children.description: World's largest tree frog at up to 14 cm; loud barking call.

children.name: Litoria aurea
children.description: A striking green-and-gold frog of southeastern Australia, once abundant but now Vulnerable after dramatic declines from chytrid fungus and habitat loss. Unusually active by day and basks in the sun.

children.name: Litoria ewingii
children.description: A small adaptable tree frog of southeastern Australia and Tasmania, also introduced to New Zealand. Its long whirring trill is a common night sound around gardens and wetlands.

children.name: Litoria gracilenta
children.description: A slender bright-green frog with yellow on the thighs and feet, found along the eastern Australian coast. Frequently encountered in gardens and bananas, earning the nickname 'graceful tree frog'.

children.name: Litoria chloris
children.description: Brilliant green frog with crimson eyes from the Australian east coast.

children.name: Litoria fallax
children.description: A tiny 2-cm green tree frog abundant along the eastern Australian coast.

children.name: Litoria booroolongensis
children.description: An Endangered stream-dwelling frog from the highlands of NSW and Victoria.
